story:
Adam Carr, a fifty-year-old antique dealer, stood in his bustling emporium, watching collectors pore over statues, jewelry, rare books, and artifacts no other dealer seemed able to find. Sir Eric Morgan shouldered through the crowd, holding a gold necklace and a dagger. He showed Adam a catalog that matched the necklace to Nefertiti’s era in ancient Egypt, circa the 14th century BC, and asked if it was a replica. Adam, calm and confident, told him to take both pieces to any expert he chose. Morgan agreed, surprised but intrigued, and left with the items.

After closing, Adam tallied a strong day—one hundred thousand dollars in sales—and decided he needed to “acquire” more antiques. In his office, he opened a hidden closet and dressed in a sleek black jumpsuit with gloves, hood, and soft shoes—no skin visible. A backpack went on his shoulders. Standing before an ornate full-length mirror, he watched as, after a few moments, he vanished from sight. Invisible, he stepped out and startled a thief who was prying at a showcase. Two sharp slaps from an unseen hand sent the man fleeing in terror. “Being invisible has its advantages,” Adam murmured, and returned to the mirror.

“Mirror, I want to see Nero watching Rome burn,” he said. The glass rippled and showed a city in flames, a man on a rooftop singing and playing a cithara. “If I don’t get it, it will be lost in the flames,” Adam said. He stepped through the mirror, crossed to Nero, and snatched the instrument. A portal opened at his command. He slipped back into his office holding the cithara, a treasure that would astonish even Sir Morgan.

A week later, Morgan returned, astonished that his flown-in expert had declared both the necklace and the dagger authentic. He paid a million for each and pressed Adam for his secret. Adam refused. Then Morgan made a special request: Nero’s “fiddle.” Adam corrected the legend: Nero didn’t have a violin—he played a cithara. He led Morgan to a showcase. “There it is.” Morgan, beyond skepticism now, bought it for two million.

Then Morgan asked for something nearly impossible—the forbidden fruit from the story of Adam and Eve. Adam called it a myth but agreed to try. That night, suited and invisible again, he faced the mirror. “Take me to the Garden of Eden when Eve encounters the serpent.” The glass opened on a lush garden. As he stepped through, his suit vanished; he stood naked among trees and sunlight. A woman turned. “Adam, come see what the serpent has for us to eat,” she said, reaching toward the fruit. Whether it was an apple or not hardly mattered.

No one saw Adam Carr again. Customers whispered, competitors speculated, and Sir Eric Morgan—who had paid fortunes for relics no one else could find—kept his suspicions to himself. Perhaps Adam had learned too late that some treasures should remain in their own time. Or perhaps, stepping through a mirror in search of a story, he had walked into the story that had been waiting for him all along.
